response_text:
```json
[
  {
    "primary_section": "Introduction",
    "primary_quote": "The key issue is that the current text-only interface to language models does not expose the model's internal computation, which produces signals that distinguish grounded generation from fabrication. This observability gap has direct consequences for verification cost. A supervisor receiving only text must spend its verification budget reconstructing, from the output alone, what the model's own computation already knew. The budget is consumed by reconstruction rather than concentrated on the cases where verification is most needed.",
    "echo_section": "The Impossibility",
    "echo_quote": "The constraint is not capability but interface or, in cost terms, the wrong investment tier. Text-only observation lacks the degrees of freedom to verify epistemic state, regardless of what the model internally represents. No amount of capability improvement can escape a verification impossibility: the model may know perfectly well that it is fabricating, but the interface provides no channel through which a supervisor can verify this knowledge independently.",
    "adds_anything": false,
    "what_it_adds": null,
    "severity": "MEDIUM",
    "estimated_lines_saveable": 5,
    "action": "SHORTEN_TO_REFERENCE",
    "explanation": "The echo in Section 3 restates the core problem from the introduction in different words before beginning the formal proof. This is unnecessary setup; a brief reference to the problem statement in the introduction would suffice."
  },
  {
    "primary_section": "Background & Motivation",
    "primary_quote": "The model can control its testimony; under current training objectives, it cannot independently control its telemetry. From a cost perspective: testimony is cheap to produce and cheap to inspect but unreliable; telemetry is more expensive to export and process but, under current training regimes, harder to game because it is a byproduct of the computation rather than a product of the model's optimization objective. This decoupling is an empirical property of existing training pipelines, not an architectural guarantee...",
    "echo_section": "Epistemic Observability",
    "echo_quote": "This interface exports telemetry that the model cannot separately control under current training objectives. The model can lie in its text, but it cannot present a different inference computation than the one it actually performed... This asymmetry is the foundation of the adversarial robustness argument: under current training objectives, computational signals (entropy, attention geometry) cannot be independently controlled by the model without changing the generation process itself, unlike behavioral signals (response length, hedging patterns) that can be adapted through training...",
    "adds_anything": true,
    "what_it_adds": "It frames the claim as the 'adversarial robustness argument' and explicitly contrasts computational signals with behavioral ones.",
    "severity": "HIGH",
    "estimated_lines_saveable": 6,
    "action": "MERGE_INTO_PRIMARY",
    "explanation": "The core claim that telemetry is an uncontrollable byproduct is stated in full in both sections. The echo in Section 4 adds useful framing ('adversarial robustness'), which should be merged into the primary definition in Section 2 to create a single, comprehensive explanation."
  },
  {
    "primary_section": "Background & Motivation",
    "primary_quote": "The self-report baseline is particularly instructive. Self-reported confidence is inverted: all four models report higher confidence on unknowable queries than on knowable ones, yielding AUC 0.28–0.36 across architectures (all below random). Tensor-based discrimination is both correctly oriented and stable (AUC 0.89–0.95) across the same four architectures. A naive user who asks “how confident are you?” receives a number that anticorrelates with actual epistemic grounding, while an adversary could exploit this inversion to make fabricated outputs appear maximally confident.",
    "echo_section": "Evaluation",
    "echo_quote": "Self-reported confidence. Self-reported confidence is inverted across all four architectures (AUC 0.28–0.36, all below random; see sec:bg_eval).",
    "adds_anything": false,
    "what_it_adds": null,
    "severity": "HIGH",
    "estimated_lines_saveable": 3,
    "action": "DELETE_ECHO",
    "explanation": "The exact same numerical result (AUC 0.28–0.36 for self-reported confidence) is presented in full in both the Background and Evaluation sections. The echo in the Evaluation section is entirely redundant and should be deleted."
  },
  {
    "primary_section": "Background & Motivation",
    "primary_quote": "The model can control its testimony; under current training objectives, it cannot independently control its telemetry. From a cost perspective: testimony is cheap to produce and cheap to inspect but unreliable; telemetry is more expensive to export and process but, under current training regimes, harder to game because it is a byproduct of the computation rather than a product of the model's optimization objective.",
    "echo_section": "Evaluation",
    "echo_quote": "This is the impossibility result instantiated empirically. Text-channel features carry behavioral signals that vary across training procedures and query distributions... Per-token entropy captures a computational property, the model's distributional uncertainty over next tokens, that the model cannot independently control under standard training objectives, the same structural pattern that makes code plagiarism detection (MOSS) robust [citation].",
    "adds_anything": true,
    "what_it_adds": "It connects the claim directly to the empirical results of the confound analysis and adds a useful analogy to MOSS plagiarism detection.",
    "severity": "MEDIUM",
    "estimated_lines_saveable": 5,
    "action": "SHORTEN_TO_REFERENCE",
    "explanation": "After demonstrating that text features fail while entropy succeeds, the paper restates the core 'uncontrollable telemetry' argument. The argument itself is repeated and should be shortened to a reference (e.g., 'This confirms the testimony/telemetry asymmetry from Sec 2.1...')."
  },
  {
    "primary_section": "The Impossibility",
    "primary_quote": "...the architecture it describes (query in, text out, no exported logits) matches the dominant LLM-as-a-service interface, and recent API changes have moved to further restrict log-probability access for newer reasoning models. If this impossibility were well-understood, we would expect interface designs to reflect it; instead, the industry is expanding operational observability (traces, spans, latency logs) while narrowing epistemic observability.",
    "echo_section": "Limitations and Future Work",
    "echo_quote": "The tensor signals we measure are byproducts of inference that providers can expose or withhold. In practice, access has eroded as models have grown more capable: log-probabilities were available on early completion endpoints but remain unsupported for the newest reasoning models, which is precisely the systems whose outputs are hardest to verify. Responsibility Concentration (cor:responsibility) formalizes the consequence: when the provider retains exclusive access to epistemic telemetry, only the provider can verify epistemic honesty.",
    "adds_anything": true,
    "what_it_adds": "It connects the observation to the 'Responsibility Concentration' corollary and frames it as a policy decision for providers, which is a key point for the Limitations section.",
    "severity": "MEDIUM",
    "estimated_lines_saveable": 4,
    "action": "MERGE_INTO_PRIMARY",
    "explanation": "The observation that API providers are restricting access to log-probs appears in both sections. The echo in Section 6 adds important context about provider policy. The two statements should be merged into a single, stronger point in Section 6, and the mention in Section 3 can be removed."
  }
]
```

SUMMARY:
- **Total redundancies found:** 5 (2 HIGH, 3 MEDIUM)
- **Estimated total lines saveable:** 23
- **The single worst offender:** The repetition of the inverted self-confidence result (AUC 0.28–0.36), which is stated in full with the exact same numbers in both Section 2 (Background) and Section 5 (Evaluation). The echo in Section 5 is entirely superfluous.
- **Particularly clean sections:** Section 7 (Related Work) is exemplary, using cross-references to compare the paper's contributions to prior art without restating its own claims unnecessarily. Section 8 (Conclusion) is also appropriately concise.

findings.primary_quote: The key issue is that the current text-only interface to language models does not expose the model's internal computation, which produces signals that distinguish grounded generation from fabrication. This observability gap has direct consequences for verification cost. A supervisor receiving only text must spend its verification budget reconstructing, from the output alone, what the model's own computation already knew. The budget is consumed by reconstruction rather than concentrated on the cases where verification is most needed.
findings.echo_quote: The constraint is not capability but interface or, in cost terms, the wrong investment tier. Text-only observation lacks the degrees of freedom to verify epistemic state, regardless of what the model internally represents. No amount of capability improvement can escape a verification impossibility: the model may know perfectly well that it is fabricating, but the interface provides no channel through which a supervisor can verify this knowledge independently.
findings.explanation: The echo in Section 3 restates the core problem from the introduction in different words before beginning the formal proof. This is unnecessary setup; a brief reference to the problem statement in the introduction would suffice.

findings.primary_quote: The model can control its testimony; under current training objectives, it cannot independently control its telemetry. From a cost perspective: testimony is cheap to produce and cheap to inspect but unreliable; telemetry is more expensive to export and process but, under current training regimes, harder to game because it is a byproduct of the computation rather than a product of the model's optimization objective. This decoupling is an empirical property of existing training pipelines, not an architectural guarantee...
findings.echo_quote: This interface exports telemetry that the model cannot separately control under current training objectives. The model can lie in its text, but it cannot present a different inference computation than the one it actually performed... This asymmetry is the foundation of the adversarial robustness argument: under current training objectives, computational signals (entropy, attention geometry) cannot be independently controlled by the model without changing the generation process itself, unlike behavioral signals (response length, hedging patterns) that can be adapted through training...
findings.what_it_adds: It frames the claim as the 'adversarial robustness argument' and explicitly contrasts computational signals with behavioral ones.
findings.explanation: The core claim that telemetry is an uncontrollable byproduct is stated in full in both sections. The echo in Section 4 adds useful framing ('adversarial robustness'), which should be merged into the primary definition in Section 2 to create a single, comprehensive explanation.

findings.primary_quote: The self-report baseline is particularly instructive. Self-reported confidence is inverted: all four models report higher confidence on unknowable queries than on knowable ones, yielding AUC 0.28–0.36 across architectures (all below random). Tensor-based discrimination is both correctly oriented and stable (AUC 0.89–0.95) across the same four architectures. A naive user who asks “how confident are you?” receives a number that anticorrelates with actual epistemic grounding, while an adversary could exploit this inversion to make fabricated outputs appear maximally confident.

findings.explanation: The exact same numerical result (AUC 0.28–0.36 for self-reported confidence) is presented in full in both the Background and Evaluation sections. The echo in the Evaluation section is entirely redundant and should be deleted.

findings.primary_quote: The model can control its testimony; under current training objectives, it cannot independently control its telemetry. From a cost perspective: testimony is cheap to produce and cheap to inspect but unreliable; telemetry is more expensive to export and process but, under current training regimes, harder to game because it is a byproduct of the computation rather than a product of the model's optimization objective.
findings.echo_quote: This is the impossibility result instantiated empirically. Text-channel features carry behavioral signals that vary across training procedures and query distributions... findings.explanation: After demonstrating that text features fail while entropy succeeds, the paper restates the core 'uncontrollable telemetry' argument. The argument itself is repeated and should be shortened to a reference (e.g., 'This confirms the testimony/telemetry asymmetry from Sec 2.1...').

findings.primary_quote: ...the architecture it describes (query in, text out, no exported logits) matches the dominant LLM-as-a-service interface, and recent API changes have moved to further restrict log-probability access for newer reasoning models. If this impossibility were well-understood, we would expect interface designs to reflect it; instead, the industry is expanding operational observability (traces, spans, latency logs) while narrowing epistemic observability.
findings.echo_quote: The tensor signals we measure are byproducts of inference that providers can expose or withhold. In practice, access has eroded as models have grown more capable: log-probabilities were available on early completion endpoints but remain unsupported for the newest reasoning models, which is precisely the systems whose outputs are hardest to verify. Responsibility Concentration (cor:responsibility) formalizes the consequence: when the provider retains exclusive access to epistemic telemetry, only the provider can verify epistemic honesty.

- **Particularly clean sections:** Section 7 (Related Work) is exemplary, using cross-references to compare the paper's contributions to prior art without restating its own claims unnecessarily. Section 8 (Conclusion) is also appropriately concise.
